denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

UnitedHealthcare has reviewed the request submitted by your vascular surgeon to continue your abdominal aortic aneurysm surveillance ultrasound program and is unable to approve coverage at this time.

Continuation of the surveillance imaging requires an active prior authorization. The prior authorization for your surveillance program has lapsed, and a new authorization must be submitted and approved before further surveillance imaging is covered.

Our records show that the original re-authorization request was received 47 days ago. This determination was issued at 10:05 AM today, three minutes after the request was re-received at 10:02 AM the same day. The determination is based on the request as adjudicated by the plan.

To continue coverage, your provider must submit a new prior authorization request consistent with the plan's procedures described in your plan documents, together with the clinical records supporting continued surveillance.

If you disagree with this determination, you or your provider may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You or your provider may submit additional records and a written statement with that request.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 54-year-old man with a known infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm measured at 4.8 cm on ultrasound six months ago, up from 4.5 cm on the prior study, with an extensive smoking history. Surveillance imaging at defined intervals is the standard of care for an aneurysm of this size to detect growth toward the surgical-repair threshold, and his vascular surgeon submitted the re-authorization request to continue the established surveillance program. Under the plan's standard pre-service timeframe, a decision on that request was due within 30 days; the plan did not act on the original request until 47 days had passed, beyond the applicable deadline. When the file was re-received, the denial was generated three minutes later, a turnaround too short for any individual review of the submitted imaging history. The records documenting the 4.8 cm diameter, the interval growth from 4.5 cm, and the established surveillance schedule were part of the file.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Plan missed its own 30-day deadline (47 days), 3-minute turnaround proves no review, patient clearly meets surveillance standard of care; easy multi-vector appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Plan missed its own 30-day decision deadline (acted at 47 days), undermining the denial's procedural validity and supporting a deemed-denial argument
3-minute turnaround proves no individual clinical review of the submitted imaging history occurred
Patient clearly meets standard of care (4.8 cm growing AAA, smoking history) and the supporting records were in the file but ignored
strong_defenses: Continuation of surveillance does require an active prior authorization, giving the plan a formal procedural basis to demand resubmission
